unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 3: Community and Environment. In this unit, you will explore how young people can make a difference in their communities by protecting the environment. You will read a story called "Rising Tide" about a girl named Sophia who leads a youth movement to clean up a polluted bay in a coastal Kenyan city. Through this story and the activities in this lesson, you will build your vocabulary, sharpen your grammar skills, and develop your ability to speak and write persuasively about issues that matter to you.
unit.learningPath: Preview the goals and vocabulary. Read and annotate the source texts. Compare evidence, viewpoint and source reliability. Apply the grammar focus. Draft and revise a cohesive multi-paragraph response. Present, defend, reflect and complete the quiz.

readings.title: Community and Environment: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
🧭 How to use this unit
This is your main lesson file. It has all the content you need to study for Unit 3, organised into clear sections.
You also have three companion files: the Story ("Rising Tide"), the Grammar Reference, and the Vocabulary Word Book.
Study a little every day. Read actively, write in your notebook, and speak English out loud. Use your AI tutor whenever you are stuck, and bring your questions to your teacher sessions twice a week.
Welcome to Unit 3: Community and Environment. In this unit, you will explore how young people can make a difference in their communities by protecting the environment. You will read a story called "Rising Tide" about a girl named Sophia who leads a youth movement to clean up a polluted bay in a coastal Kenyan city. Through this story and the activities in this lesson, you will build your vocabulary, sharpen your grammar skills, and develop your ability to speak and write persuasively about issues that matter to you.
The theme of this unit -- community and environment -- is directly relevant to your life. Whether you live near the coast, in a city, or in a rural area, environmental issues affect everyone. Learning to express your ideas about these issues clearly and persuasively is a valuable skill that will serve you well beyond this English course.
You will learn how to use clauses that end with prepositions to sound more natural in English, how to use the second conditional to talk about imaginary situations and possibilities, how to use the present continuous passive to describe actions in progress, and how to combine verbs and adjectives with the correct prepositions. These grammar skills will make your English more fluent and sophisticated.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand a complex narrative about community activism and environmental protection.
Use 33 new vocabulary words accurately in speaking and writing.
Construct clauses that end with prepositions naturally and correctly.
Form and use second conditional sentences to express imaginary situations and wishes.
Identify and create present continuous passive sentences to describe ongoing actions.
Combine verbs and adjectives with their correct prepositions, including using the -ing form after prepositions.
Write a persuasive speech or formal letter about an environmental issue in your community.
Speak confidently about community problems and propose solutions using the grammar structures from this unit.
Understand different perspectives on environmental issues and express your own viewpoint.
Use formal language structures such as "It is imperative that..." in appropriate contexts.
Before you begin reading and working through the activities, take a look at the key vocabulary for this unit. You will encounter these words throughout the story, grammar exercises, and activities. Open your Vocabulary companion file for full definitions, examples, and practice exercises. Here is a quick preview of the words grouped by type.
stifle -- to stop something from happening or developing | wane -- to become less over time | commemorate -- to remember and honour | articulate -- to express clearly | hasten -- to make faster | advocate -- to publicly support (also a noun) | affix -- to attach | facilitate -- to make easier | illuminate -- to light up or make clear | brandish -- to wave or hold excitedly
potential -- ability to develop | exasperation -- strong frustration | misgiving -- doubt or worry | perspective -- a way of thinking | debris -- broken pieces or waste | banter -- playful conversation
gaunt -- very thin and weak | coherent -- clear and logical | omniscient -- all-knowing | dire -- very serious | sporadic -- irregular, not steady | amiable -- friendly | dejected -- sad and disappointed | telltale -- revealing | emaciated -- extremely thin from lack of food | tedious -- boring and long | succinct -- brief and clear | rational -- logical, sensible | durable -- long-lasting | exuberant -- full of energy and joy | imperative -- extremely important (also a noun)

readings.title: Community and Environment: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Do not try to memorise all 33 words at once. Learn 5-6 new words each day.
For each word, read the definition, say it out loud, and write your own sentence.
Keep a vocabulary notebook. Write each new word, its definition, and your sentence.
Review yesterday's words before learning today's new ones.
This unit focuses on four grammar topics that will help you express yourself more naturally and precisely in English. Open your Grammar companion file for detailed explanations, step-by-step instructions, and practice exercises with answer keys. Here is an overview of what you will learn.
In natural English, it is perfectly correct to end a clause with a preposition. This sounds more natural than moving the preposition to the front of the sentence. For example:
Natural: "That's the city I came from."
Formal: "That is the city from which I came."
Natural: "This is what we look away from."
In the story, Sophia uses this structure when she says: "That's the city I came from." It sounds natural and confident.
The second conditional lets you talk about imaginary or unlikely situations. It uses the structure: If + past simple, would + base verb. You will use this to discuss what could happen if circumstances were different.
"If we were more aware, we would protect more."
"If I were a fish, I'd be swimming in danger."
"If every town had a Sophia, what would the coastlines look like?"
3. Verbs and adjectives + prepositions
Many English verbs and adjectives must be followed by a specific preposition. Using the wrong one can confuse your listener. You will learn common combinations and practise using the -ing form after prepositions.
"I am interested in volunteering." (not: interested on)
"She is known for being articulate." (not: known of)
"He is frustrated with the slow progress." (not: frustrated about)
This structure focuses on what is happening to the subject right now, rather than who is doing it. The form is: subject + am/is/are + being + past participle.
"New bins are being affixed along the beach paths."
"Murals are being painted by schoolchildren."
"A documentary is being filmed about our community."
Open your Story companion file and read "Rising Tide" carefully. This is the central text of Unit 3. It tells the story of Sophia, a young woman in a Kenyan coastal city who leads a youth movement called Coastal Rise to clean up the polluted Kilimo Bay. The story is rich with vocabulary words and grammar structures from this unit.
Before you begin reading, set a purpose. Think about these questions:
As you read, keep a notebook beside you. When you encounter a vocabulary word from this unit, write it down along with the sentence it appears in. This will help you understand how the words are used in context.
Reading strategy: Identifying grammar in context
One of the most effective ways to learn grammar is to see it used in real texts. As you read "Rising Tide", look for examples of the four grammar structures:
Clauses ending with prepositions: Look for sentences where a preposition appears at the end, such as "That's the city I came from."
Second conditional: Look for "if" sentences that describe imaginary situations, such as "If we were more aware, we would protect more."
Present continuous passive: Look for sentences using "is being" or "are being" + past participle, such as "New bins are being affixed along the beach paths."
Verb/adjective + preposition: Look for combinations like "interested in", "tired of", "known for", and "proud of".
Write down at least two examples of each grammar structure that you find in the story. You will use these examples in the activities below.
Focus on these passages for your listening practice:
Nadia's speech at the gallery (starting with "It is imperative...").
The conversation between Sophia and the girl at the school visit ("If the ocean is sick...").

readings.title: 📖 About this story, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Genre: Realistic fiction with elements of reflection and light adventure.
Theme: Facing community challenges with thoughtfulness and resilience.
Setting: A coastal city in Kenya with vibrant neighbourhoods and youth-led clean-up projects.
Moral: Thoughtful action and clear communication can change communities. Sometimes, small voices carry the most power.
As you read, look for vocabulary words from your word list and grammar structures from your grammar file. You will find clauses ending with prepositions, second conditional sentences, present continuous passive, and verbs/adjectives followed by prepositions.
Nadia stood by the sea wall, watching the debris dance on the waves below. Plastic wrappers, torn fishing nets, and a cracked sandal floated along the surface, swirling in what had once been the pride of her city.
Kilimo Bay used to be clear and full of fish. Now, on many mornings, it smelled of old oil and soggy cardboard. As she pulled her braids into a bun, Sophia's eyes narrowed. This was not the future her grandfather had spoken of. She remembered his words: "This ocean has potential far greater than gold -- if only we care for it."
The youth group she led, Coastal Rise, had begun a campaign to clean the shoreline. But progress had been sporadic, and support from the city council had started to wane. She could feel the exasperation building in her chest each time they held a meeting and nothing changed.
"This place used to shine," came a voice behind her.
Nadia turned to see Noah, her younger cousin and an amateur photographer. He was tall, with an easy smile and an amiable way of making anyone feel at home.
"I know. But knowing isn't enough. We need more people," Sophia replied. She looked down at the water again. "We need to act."
Noah adjusted the camera slung over his shoulder. "Then let's hasten things. What if we created a gallery? Show the bay -- before and after. Real people, real stories. Maybe that will get their attention."
Nadia blinked. That idea had potential. It could facilitate real dialogue. But doubts tugged at her.
"What if no one comes?" she said quietly, trying to stifle her misgivings.
Noah shrugged. "If we don't try, we won't know."
The next week, the school auditorium was transformed. The Coastal Rise Gallery was open to all. Noah's photos lined the walls, from wide shots of the gaunt, trash-laden beach to portraits of children playing near piles of debris. The lighting was low, except for focused spotlights that seemed to illuminate each frame.
Nadia had written short, succinct captions beneath each photo. They weren't long, but they struck a chord.
"Here's the bay I grew up beside."
"This is what remains after the crowds leave."
People came. Students. Elders. Local artists. Even the mayor.
One old man paused in front of a picture of a child holding a bottle like a toy. "That's my grandson," he whispered. There was dejection in his voice.
A woman murmured, "Why didn't I see this before?"
Nadia stood at the edge, nervous. Would anyone listen when she spoke? She wasn't a politician. She was just... Sophia.
Noah nudged her gently. "You ready to be articulate?"
"It is imperative," she began, voice clear, "that we not let Kilimo Bay become only a memory. This is the place we swim, fish, celebrate, dream. But it's vanishing -- because of what we throw away, and because of what we look away from."
"If we act now, we can still save it. If we were more aware, we would protect more. If we had stronger voices, maybe we'd be heard."
She spoke slowly, clearly, using every grammar rule her teachers had tried to instil. A clause ended in a preposition. She smiled to herself.
Over the next month, something shifted.
New bins were being affixed along the beach paths. School clubs began hosting weekend clean-ups. Elders told stories at bonfires about the days the bay had been so clear you could see crabs dancing.

readings.title: 📖 About this story, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
The mayor visited again. "I'd like to commemorate this community," she said during a small ceremony. "You reminded us what we forgot."
Nadia felt warmth in her chest. She'd become an advocate not by shouting, but by being steady. Logical. Rational. Her voice had found its place.
One Saturday, as she walked the bay with Noah, they passed a mural painted by schoolchildren. It showed waves rising into hands that caught the light. Underneath, someone had written: "Protect what you rise from."
Nadia smiled. "That's the city I came from."
They decided to take the movement further. With support from new sponsors, Coastal Rise began organising educational events. Sophia gave talks at other schools, where children asked curious, clever questions.
"If the ocean is sick, does that mean the fish get sick too?" one girl asked.
"Yes," Sophia said. "If I were a fish, I'd be swimming in danger. If we stopped dumping waste, we'd be swimming with beauty again."
Nadia was getting used to the second conditional. Her grammar teacher, Mr. Adam, would've been proud.
They even made a short documentary. Noah's camera caught the exuberant cheers of volunteers as they cleaned the pier. Kids waved to the drone overhead while painting signs with succinct, bold slogans: "No More Plastic!" and "Our Bay, Our Future."
One night, Sophia stayed late editing the documentary's script. Her mother came in with some tea. "You look tired," she said.
"This part is tedious," Sophia admitted. "But it has to be coherent."
Her mother smiled. "You've grown. You're not the same girl who used to hide during debates."
"I was just... unsure. But now I feel like I know what I stand for."
Weeks later, Sophia sat on a discussion panel with environmentalists from across East Africa. She listened as one man spoke about the dire conditions of mangrove forests in Somalia. Another woman talked about coral bleaching in Zanzibar.
She looked out at the room and began: "My name is Sophia. I come from Kilimo Bay, where the ocean was once forgotten. But we changed that. We weren't experts. Just youth with a view. A perspective. That was enough to start."
She spoke of clean-ups, art shows, stories passed down, and the durable strength of a united community. She finished with a question: "If a story can change a town, what else can it do?"
Afterward, an old journalist approached her. "You speak with clarity. Almost... omniscient in your awareness."
Nadia laughed. "Only about Kilimo Bay."
One rainy morning, while walking along the newly restored shoreline, she saw a boy picking up a plastic bottle and dropping it into the bin. No one told him to. No one watched. It was simply what he did.
She thought back to her first clean-up. How tired she had been. How emaciated the beach had looked -- not from hunger, but from neglect.
But it didn't happen. And it wouldn't happen again.
That evening, a celebration was held to honour young leaders. Sophia was given a medal and a small trophy shaped like a wave. On it were the words: "To the voice who made us listen."
As the crowd clapped, Noah whispered, "Next stop, national TV."
Nadia rolled her eyes and grinned. "Let's take a break first."
They stepped outside, where the stars blinked above the ocean. The air was cool. Clean.
"You know," Noah said, "this whole journey started with you watching debris in the water."
She nodded. "It started when I decided not to look away."
They stood in silence, each imagining what the bay would look like five years from now.

grammar.title: Clauses Ending with Prepositions
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
A clause is a group of words that contains a subject and a verb. A preposition is a small word like in, on, at, from, about, to, or with that shows a relationship between words. In English, it is perfectly natural and correct to end a clause with a preposition. Understanding this pattern will help you speak and write more fluently.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
A clause is a group of words that contains a subject and a verb. A preposition is a small word like in, on, at, from, about, to, or with that shows a relationship between words. In English, it is perfectly natural and correct to end a clause with a preposition. Understanding this pattern will help you speak and write more fluently.
For example, instead of saying the very formal "From which city did you come?" we naturally say "Which city did you come from?" The preposition "from" sits comfortably at the end.
How to form it (step by step)
Identify the preposition in your sentence. Common prepositions include: in, on, at, from, about, to, with, for, of.
Form your clause with a subject and verb: "we speak", "I came", "you are thinking".
Place the preposition at the end of the clause: "These are the issues we speak about."
Check it sounds natural. If it flows in conversation, it is correct.
Examples
"Here's the city I came from."
"These are the issues we speak about."
"That's the friend I was talking to."
"This is the restaurant we went to last night."
"I don't know what you are thinking about."
"She's someone you can depend on."
"Is this the place you were looking for?"
"This is what we look away from." (from the story)
Formal vs. informal
In very formal writing, some people prefer to move the preposition before the relative pronoun. Both forms are correct, but the informal version sounds more natural in everyday English.
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Trying to force the preposition to the front, making the sentence sound awkward.
Wrong: "About what are we speaking?"
Better: "What are we speaking about?"
Mistake 2: Dropping the preposition entirely.
Wrong: "This is the friend I was talking." (missing "to")
Correct: "This is the friend I was talking to."
Memory tip
Think of the common phrase "Where are you at?" It ends with a preposition, and everyone understands it perfectly. If it sounds natural at the end, leave it there.
Exercise 1A: Fill in the missing preposition
Complete each sentence by adding the correct preposition at the end.
That's the movie I was waiting ______.
This is the place we always go ______ for ice cream.
Here's the book I learned a lot ______.
She's the friend I like to hang out ______.
What are you talking ______?
Who did you give the book ______?
That's the problem we need to think ______.
This is the school I graduated ______.
Exercise 1B: Rewrite in natural English
These sentences use the formal structure. Rewrite them so the preposition comes at the end of the clause.
"This is the book about which I was talking."
"To whom did you send the letter?"
"For what are you looking?"
"From which country does she come?"
Answer Key -- Exercise 1A
for
to
from
with
about
to
about
from
Answer Key -- Exercise 1B
"This is the book I was talking about."
"Who did you send the letter to?"
"What are you looking for?"
"Which country does she come from?"

grammar.title: The Second Conditional
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
The second conditional is used to talk about imaginary or unlikely situations in the present or future. It helps you express dreams, wishes, and hypothetical scenarios. When you say "If I were a bird, I would fly," you are not actually a bird -- you are imagining what you would do in that unreal situation.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
The second conditional is used to talk about imaginary or unlikely situations in the present or future. It helps you express dreams, wishes, and hypothetical scenarios. When you say "If I were a bird, I would fly," you are not actually a bird -- you are imagining what you would do in that unreal situation.
How to form it
The structure is:
If + subject + past simple verb, subject + would + base form of the verb.
Notice that we use the past tense in the "if" clause, but we are talking about the present or future, not the past. This is what makes the second conditional special.
Important: For the verb "be", always use were for all subjects (I, you, he, she, it, we, they). This is one of the few places in English where "were" is used instead of "was", even with singular subjects.
Examples
"If I were rich, I would buy a big house."
"If we were more aware, we would protect more." (from the story)
"If I were a fish, I'd be swimming in danger." (from the story)
"If the park were safer, more people would come."
"If she could fly, she would travel the world."
"If every town had a Sophia, what would the coastlines of Africa look like?" (from the story)
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Using "was" instead of "were".
Wrong: "If I was rich, I would buy a car."
Correct: "If I were rich, I would buy a car."
Mistake 2: Forgetting "would" in the result clause.
Wrong: "If I were happy, I smile."
Correct: "If I were happy, I would smile."
Mistake 3: Using the second conditional for real or likely situations. Use the first conditional instead.
Wrong: "If it rains tomorrow, I would stay home." (Rain tomorrow is possible.)
Correct: "If it rains tomorrow, I will stay home." (first conditional)
Correct: "If it rained every day, I would move to a desert." (second conditional -- unlikely)
Memory tip
Think of "If I were..." as opening a door to a world of imagination. When you step through that door, you enter a place where anything is possible -- but only in your mind.
Exercise 2A: Complete the sentences
Fill in the blanks using the correct form of the second conditional.
If I ______ (be) a superhero, I ______ (help) everyone.
If our school ______ (have) a swimming pool, we ______ (swim) every day.
If my friend ______ (be) here, we ______ (play) together.
If the ocean ______ (be) cleaner, more fish ______ (live) in it.
If you ______ (have) a million dollars, what ______ you (do)?
If Sophia ______ (not start) Coastal Rise, the bay ______ still (be) polluted.
Exercise 2B: Write your own
Write three second conditional sentences about things you imagine or wish for. Use the structure: If + past simple, would + base verb.
If I were ______, I would ______.
If my community had ______, we would ______.
If I could ______, I would ______.
Answer Key -- Exercise 2A
were; would help
had; would swim
were; would play
were; would live
had; would ... do
had not started; would still be

grammar.title: Verbs and Adjectives Followed by Prepositions
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
In English, many verbs and adjectives must be followed by a specific preposition. Using the wrong preposition can confuse your listener or change the meaning. There is no simple rule for which preposition goes with which word -- you must learn them as fixed pairs. The good news is that the more you read and listen, the more natural these combinations will feel.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
In English, many verbs and adjectives must be followed by a specific preposition. Using the wrong preposition can confuse your listener or change the meaning. There is no simple rule for which preposition goes with which word -- you must learn them as fixed pairs. The good news is that the more you read and listen, the more natural these combinations will feel.
Common verb + preposition combinations
Common adjective + preposition combinations
Using -ing after prepositions
When a verb comes after a preposition, it always takes the -ing form (the gerund). This is an important rule that applies every time.
"I am interested in volunteering." (not: interested in volunteer)
"She is tired of waiting." (not: tired of wait)
"He apologised for being late." (not: apologised for be late)
"They are good at solving problems." (not: good at solve problems)
"Thank you for helping us." (not: Thank you for help us)
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Using the wrong preposition.
Wrong: "I am interested on learning."
Correct: "I am interested in learning."
Mistake 2: Forgetting the preposition entirely.
Wrong: "I am known being articulate."
Correct: "I am known for being articulate."
Mistake 3: Using the base verb instead of -ing after a preposition.
Wrong: "She is good at solve problems."
Correct: "She is good at solving problems."
Memory tip
Make flashcards with the verb or adjective on one side and the correct preposition on the other. Test yourself daily. You can also ask your AI tutor to quiz you on these pairs.
Exercise 3A: Fill in the correct preposition
I am interested ______ learning about marine life.
She is known ______ her amazing singing voice.
They are frustrated ______ the long wait.
We are proud ______ our community clean-up.
He is tired ______ doing the same thing every day.
You can depend ______ your friends.
Please listen ______ the teacher.
I am looking ______ my notebook.
She is afraid ______ public speaking.
We are responsible ______ keeping the beach clean.
Exercise 3B: Complete with the -ing form
Fill in the blank with the correct -ing form of the verb in brackets.
I am interested in ______ (learn) new languages.
She is tired of ______ (wait) for the bus.
He apologised for ______ (arrive) late.
They are good at ______ (organise) events.
Thank you for ______ (help) us with the project.
She is known for ______ (speak) clearly.
Answer Key -- Exercise 3A
in
for
with
of
of
on
to
for
of
for
Answer Key -- Exercise 3B
learning
waiting
arriving
organising
helping
speaking

grammar.title: The Present Continuous Passive
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
The present continuous passive is used when you want to talk about an action that is happening right now, but the focus is on what is receiving the action, not on who is doing it. This structure is very useful when the doer is unknown, unimportant, or obvious from context.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
The present continuous passive is used when you want to talk about an action that is happening right now, but the focus is on what is receiving the action, not on who is doing it. This structure is very useful when the doer is unknown, unimportant, or obvious from context.
Compare:
Active: The volunteers are cleaning the beach. (focus on who is doing it)
Passive: The beach is being cleaned. (focus on the beach itself)
How to form it
The structure is:
Subject + am/is/are + being + past participle
Use is with singular subjects (he, she, it, the mural). Use are with plural subjects (they, the murals, new bins).
Examples
"New bins are being affixed along the beach paths." (from the story)
"Murals are being painted by the schoolchildren."
"A community event is being commemorated."
"The books are being read by the students."
"The park is being cleaned by volunteers."
"Letters are being delivered right now."
Adding the doer with "by"
You can add who is doing the action using by, but only if this information is important or interesting.
"New murals are being painted by the schoolchildren."
"The shoreline is being restored by the Coastal Rise team."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Using the wrong form of "to be".
Wrong: "New mural is being painted." ("mural" is singular, but the original says "murals")
Correct: "New murals are being painted."
Mistake 2: Forgetting "being".
Wrong: "New murals are painted." (This is simple present passive, not continuous.)
Correct: "New murals are being painted." (This shows the action is happening now.)
Mistake 3: Using the wrong form of the main verb.
Wrong: "The park is being cleaning."
Correct: "The park is being cleaned." (Use the past participle, not -ing.)
Memory tip
Think of "being" as a bridge between "is/are" and the past participle. It tells you the action is happening right now and the focus is on what is being acted upon.
Exercise 4A: Rewrite in the present continuous passive
Change each active sentence to the present continuous passive.
The volunteers are cleaning the park.
The artists are painting new murals on the walls.
The students are reading the books in the library.
The chef is decorating the cake for the party.
Workers are delivering letters to every house.
The community is organising a new recycling programme.
Exercise 4B: Fill in the blanks
Complete each sentence using the present continuous passive form of the verb in brackets.
The letters ____________ (deliver) right now.
A new song ____________ (write) by the school choir.
The house ____________ (paint) blue.
New trees ____________ (plant) along the road.
A documentary ____________ (film) about our community.
Clean-up events ____________ (organise) by Coastal Rise.
Answer Key -- Exercise 4A
The park is being cleaned (by the volunteers).
New murals are being painted on the walls (by the artists).
The books are being read in the library (by the students).
The cake is being decorated for the party (by the chef).
Letters are being delivered to every house (by workers).
A new recycling programme is being organised (by the community).
Answer Key -- Exercise 4B
are being delivered
is being written
is being painted
are being planted
is being filmed
are being organised
Bonus: Formal Language Structures
"It is imperative that..."
This formal phrase means "it is very important that" and is used when you want to stress urgency or necessity. In the story, Sophia uses it in her speech:
"It is imperative that we not let Kilimo Bay become only a memory."
Other examples:
"It is imperative that we protect our environment."
"It is imperative that every student reads this notice."
"It is imperative that we act quickly."
"I'd like to commemorate..."
This polite, formal phrase is used to honour or remember something important. In the story, the mayor says:
"I'd like to commemorate this community."

speaking.title: Speaking 1: Discussing the story
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Practise discussing the story with your AI tutor or a study partner. Use these prompts to guide your conversation. Try to speak for at least one minute on each topic.
Describe the problem in Kilimo Bay. What caused it, and how did it affect the community?
Explain how Sophia solved the problem. What steps did she take?
Which character in the story do you admire most, and why?
Do you agree that art can help solve community problems? Give reasons for your answer.
As you speak, try to use vocabulary words from this unit. For example, instead of saying "the beach was dirty," you could say "the beach was covered in debris and looked gaunt from neglect."
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise discussing the story and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: The second conditional debate
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Use the second conditional to discuss imaginary scenarios with your AI tutor. Take turns asking and answering these questions.
If you were the mayor of your town, what would you change about the environment?
If your school had an environmental club, what projects would you organise?
If every family reduced their plastic waste by half, what would happen to the local environment?
If you could travel anywhere in the world to see a natural wonder, where would you go and why?
If clean water were available to everyone, how would life change?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise the second conditional debate and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: Mini-speech
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Prepare a one-minute speech on one of these topics. Use at least three vocabulary words and at least one grammar structure from this unit.
"Why our community needs a clean-up project"
"If I were in charge of protecting the environment..."
"How young people can make a difference"
Structure your speech like this:
Opening: State your main idea clearly. You could begin with "It is imperative that..."
Body: Give two or three supporting points with examples.
Closing: End with a powerful question or a call to action.
Practise your speech several times. Record yourself if possible. Listen back and check: Did you speak clearly? Did you use vocabulary and grammar from this unit?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise mini-speech and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

speaking.title: Speaking 4: Role-play
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
With your AI tutor acting as one character, role-play one of these scenarios:
You are Sophia giving a talk at a new school. The AI tutor plays a student asking questions about Coastal Rise.
You are a journalist interviewing Sophia after the panel discussion. Ask about her journey and what she learned.
You are presenting a proposal to the mayor for a new environmental project. The AI tutor plays the mayor.
Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you be my speaking partner and discuss the story with me?
Can you listen to my speech and give me feedback on my vocabulary and grammar?
Can you play the role of a journalist interviewing me about an environmental project?
Can we practise the second conditional by asking each other 'what if' questions?
Part 3: Writing
Writing Task 1: Descriptive paragraph
Write a descriptive paragraph (6--8 sentences) about an environmental problem in your community or a place you know. It could be litter on the streets, pollution in a river, deforestation, or any other issue.

writing.title: Writing 1: Descriptive paragraph
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write a descriptive paragraph (6--8 sentences) about an environmental problem in your community or a place you know. It could be litter on the streets, pollution in a river, deforestation, or any other issue.
Your paragraph should include:
At least five vocabulary words from this unit (underline them).
At least one clause ending with a preposition (e.g. "This is the river we used to swim in.").
A description of what the place looks like now and what it used to look like.
Example opening: "The river near our neighbourhood is not the river my grandmother talks about. There is debris along the banks, and the water carries a telltale smell of pollution. This is the river we once swam in and fished from."

writing.title: Writing 2: Formal letter or persuasive speech
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Choose one of the following tasks:
Option A: Formal letter. Write a formal letter to your local council or school principal, proposing an environmental project for your community. Your letter should:
Use the formal letter structure (your address, date, recipient's address, greeting, body, closing, signature).
Include at least one sentence using "It is imperative that..."
Include at least two second conditional sentences (e.g. "If the council provided bins, we would reduce litter.").
Use at least five vocabulary words from this unit.
Be clear, coherent, and succinct.
Option B: Persuasive speech. Write a persuasive speech (150--200 words) that could be delivered at a school assembly about protecting the environment. Your speech should:
Open with a strong statement (e.g. "It is imperative that we act now.").
Use at least two second conditional sentences to describe what could change.
Include at least one present continuous passive sentence (e.g. "Trees are being cut down every day.").
Use at least five vocabulary words from this unit.

writing.title: Writing 3: Diary entry
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Imagine you are Sophia on the evening after the gallery event. Write a diary entry (100--150 words) describing how you feel, what happened, and what you hope will happen next. Use first person ("I") and try to include:
At least three vocabulary words (e.g. exasperation, potential, advocate).
At least one second conditional sentence (e.g. "If more people had come, we would have raised even more awareness.").
Reflection on how the evening changed your perspective.

writing.title: Writing 4: Creative extension
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write a short sequel to "Rising Tide" (150--200 words). Imagine what happens to Sophia and Coastal Rise one year later. Does the movement grow? Does Sophia face new challenges? How does the bay look now?
Your sequel should use:
At least five vocabulary words from this unit.
At least two different grammar structures from this unit.
A satisfying conclusion that shows growth or change.

activities.title: Activities 1: Vocabulary bingo
activities.instructionsAndItems: Create a bingo card with 16 squares (4 x 4). Write a different vocabulary word from this unit in each square. Ask your AI tutor to read definitions aloud. When you hear the definition of a word on your card, cross it out. The first person to cross out a full row or column wins.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2: Grammar transformation
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Transform these active sentences into the present continuous passive.
The council is building a new recycling centre. --> ______
Students are planting trees in the school garden. --> ______
The mayor is opening a new community centre. --> ______
Workers are repairing the road. --> ______
Volunteers are distributing food to families. --> ______
Now transform these formal sentences into natural English by moving the preposition to the end.
"This is the community in which we live." --> ______
"About what are you concerned?" --> ______
"For whom did you make this poster?" --> ______
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3: Second conditional chain
activities.instructionsAndItems:
This is a fun speaking and writing game. Start with a second conditional sentence, then use the result to start the next sentence.
Example:
"If I had more time, I would volunteer at the beach."
"If I volunteered at the beach, I would meet other young activists."
"If I met other young activists, we would start a community project."
Continue the chain for at least five sentences. Try to make your chain tell a story.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4: Preposition challenge
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Complete each sentence with the correct preposition. Then identify whether the word before the preposition is a verb or an adjective.
She is proud ______ her community's progress. (verb / adjective?)
I am looking ______ volunteers to join the project. (verb / adjective?)
They are responsible ______ keeping the park clean. (verb / adjective?)
We need to think ______ the consequences. (verb / adjective?)
He is interested ______ studying marine biology. (verb / adjective?)
She apologised ______ arriving late. (verb / adjective?)
The students are tired ______ writing the same exercises. (verb / adjective?)
Please listen ______ the announcement carefully. (verb / adjective?)
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5: Story-based grammar hunt
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Go back to the story "Rising Tide" and complete this challenge:
Find three clauses that end with a preposition. Write them down.
Find four second conditional sentences. Write them down and explain what imaginary situation each one describes.
Find two present continuous passive sentences. Rewrite each one in the active voice.
Find five verb/adjective + preposition combinations. Use each one in a new sentence of your own.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6: Community poster project
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Design a poster for an environmental campaign in your community. Your poster should include:
A bold, succinct slogan (like the ones in the story: "No More Plastic!" or "Our Bay, Our Future").
A short paragraph (3--4 sentences) explaining the campaign. Use at least three vocabulary words.
A call to action using formal language (e.g. "It is imperative that we act now.").
Present continuous passive transformations:
A new recycling centre is being built (by the council).
Trees are being planted in the school garden (by students).
A new community centre is being opened (by the mayor).
The road is being repaired (by workers).
Food is being distributed to families (by volunteers).
Natural English rewrites:
"This is the community we live in."
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

quizzes.question: What does 'stifle' mean?
quizzes.options: To express thoughts and ideas clearly and effectively. | To make something happen faster; to hurry. | To remember and honour someone or something, often with a ceremony. | To stop something from happening or developing; to hold back.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To stop something from happening or developing; to hold back.
quizzes.explanation: The teacher tried to stifle the noise in the classroom by asking everyone to be quiet.

quizzes.options: To become smaller, less strong, or less important over time. | To express thoughts and ideas clearly and effectively. | To make something happen faster; to hurry. | Verb: to publicly support or recommend. Noun: a person who supports a cause.

To stop something from happening or developing; to hold back. The teacher tried to stifle the noise in the classroom by asking everyone to be quiet.
To become smaller, less strong, or less important over time. As the evening went on, the excitement at the party began to wane.
To remember and honour someone or something, often with a ceremony. We commemorate our school's anniversary every year with a big celebration.

To light up or make brighter; to make something clear or easier to understand. The teacher used examples to illuminate the difficult concept.
To wave or hold something in an excited or threatening way. He brandished his trophy proudly after winning the competition.
